text: The video shows the stomach protruding into the chest cavity through a wide hiatal defect during a paraesophageal hernia repair with a Toupee fundoplication.
The surgeon dissects into the hernia sac space, pulling the hernia sac down to reveal the space between the hernia sac and the mediastinum.

The surgeon develops a space between the stomach and esophagus below and the mediastinum above, which contains the heart. On the right side, the surgeon frees the esophageal attachments, retracting the esophagus on the left and working around it to release the mediastinal attachments.
The surgeon identifies the aorta below the dissection area and proceeds to ligate the short gastric blood vessels, which are visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon frees the stomach from the diaphragm and places a Penrose drain around the backside of the stomach and gastroesophageal junction. The Penrose drain aids in retracting the esophagus and freeing attachments that keep it herniated in the chest. The procedure is performed anterior to the esophagus, creating a plane between the esophagus and the mediastinum, with the beating heart visible at the top of the screen.
The surgeon continues to free up attachments on the right posterior aspect of the esophagus, carefully working above the aorta while preserving the vagus nerve.
The surgeon addresses a wide hiatal defect by suturing the hiatus more snugly around the esophagus using a permanent braided suture at the bottom of the defect.

The surgeon performs hand suturing to snugly oppose the hiatal defect around the esophagus, ensuring it is not too tight. The stitches are primarily placed posteriorly, but the final stitch is positioned on the anterior right side. When the esophagus is left to rest, the hiatus visibly closes around it without any remaining defects.

The surgeon performs a posterior 270-degree Toupee fundoplication by bringing the gastric fundus behind the esophagus from left to right. Both sides of the fundus are grasped, and the shoeshine maneuver is executed to prevent twisting. The fundus is then sutured to the esophagus using Ethabon sutures, with the final stitch placed on the left side.
The surgeon begins suturing the fundoplication on the right side, completing the final stitch to secure the fundus to the esophagus.

The surgeon places sutures to anchor the gastroesophageal junction and fundoplication to the diaphragm. A stitch is placed from the top of the left side of the fundoplication to the diaphragm, followed by suturing the posterior aspect of the fundoplication to the posterior aspect of the crura, securing the stomach in the intra-abdominal location.
The final appearance of the toupee fundoplication is shown after completing the paraesophageal hernia repair, with the fundus wrapped around the esophagus and sutured in place.
